articles.title: RESTRICTION ON ENTRY OF CERTAIN NONIMMIGRANT WORKERS
articles.summary: A new proclamation blocks most foreign tech workers from entering the US on H-1B visas unless their employers pay a $100,000 surcharge per petition. The move responds to claims that companies have replaced Americans with cheaper labor, often forcing laid-off staff to train their replacements. Exceptions exist for “national interest” cases, but the order raises labor costs sharply and signals a push to reserve entry-level IT jobs for domestic graduates.

articles.title: NAVAN FILES TO IPO
articles.summary: Navan is heading to the public markets with $688M ARR, 30% growth, and gross margins up to 73% after major AI-driven efficiency gains. Still, net revenue retention barely clears 110%, free cash flow margins sit at -5%, and its Rule of 40 score is just 25%, near the bottom quartile. Investors will judge whether margin progress and AI-powered support can offset middling growth and a lower-revenue scale compared to top SaaS peers.

articles.title: WHAT ROBOTS CAN (AND CAN'T) DO IN 2025
articles.summary: Robots still struggle with tasks like picking up objects due to the challenges in machine perception and manipulation. Achieving breakthroughs akin to a "ChatGPT moment" in robotics requires blending advanced AI models with traditional engineering. The future of robotics depends on combining simulation data with real-world applications to overcome current limitations.

articles.title: HOW TO GET CITED IN AI ANSWERS
articles.summary: Did you know AI models cite brands mentioned at industry conferences? This SEO expert discovered it after ChatGPT kept recommending his competitors despite his better domain authority and more backlinks. Turns out LLMs scan transcribed conference talks, YouTube presentations, and LinkedIn recaps - places his competitors were getting namedropped while he bought Reddit mentions. The models also track user corrections. A pizza shop vanished from AI suggestions after enough people typed "don't show me that again" in follow-up prompts. His competitors weren't trying to game AI at all. They were presenting case studies at conferences, getting discussed in customer Slack channels, and showing up in YouTube tutorials. These mentions trained the AI to trust them.

articles.title: CLUELY THESIS ON VIRALITY AND HYPE
articles.summary: Viral content will spread if it's seen by a large enough audience and deserves attention. The true value of going viral lies in gaining user interest, especially post-PMF, rather than driving immediate conversions.

articles.title: HOW TO FIND THE PERFECT NAME
articles.summary: David Placek and his team at Lexicon Branding have named nearly 4,000 brands, including Swiffer, Sonos, and Pentium, by using a structured naming process. In today's competitive and AI-driven market, an effective name can create trust, communicate originality, and be globally accessible. The Lexicon Framework advises generating thousands of names, considering sound symbolism, and emphasizing trust and uniqueness over descriptiveness.

articles.title: THE SELF IS THE PLATFORM
articles.summary: AI has inverted the fundamental equation of technology - individuals can now rival entire teams, and private tools are beating public platforms. One person with ChatGPT and Cursor can ship what used to require 50 people. The last tech boom was about coordinating people at scale, but AI flipped it - personal leverage beats social leverage now. These aren't social networks, they're supertools that multiply individual capacity without needing anyone else. The individual is now the base unit of scale.

articles.title: HOW TO USE AI WITHOUT BECOMING STUPID
articles.summary: We trust AI to draft emails, schedule calls, even write code, but the real danger isn't mistakes — it's letting it choose for us. A simple rule has quietly emerged across classrooms, trading desks, and dev teams: never give up your value judgments. The paradox is that automation works best when it leaves humans more room to decide.

articles.title: THE IPO MARKET IS OPENING UP. THESE 14 COMPANIES COULD BE NEXT
articles.summary: Crunchbase's predictive tools spotlight 14 companies across sectors like fintech, AI, and consumer goods as probable IPO candidates. Notables include Stripe, with its $91.5 billion valuation, and Databricks, which raised a $10 billion round in 2024. Emerging sectors like cybersecurity and space tech spotlight firms like Ledger, likely to IPO due to its crypto-wallet prominence, and Sierra Space, backed by $3.4 billion in contracts.
